items.title: Who is John Ternus, the incoming Apple CEO?
items.content: Starting on September 1, Ternus will lead one of the world's most valuable companies, but if you're not a dedicated Apple enthusiast, you've probably never heard of this man,

items.title: Tim Cook stepping down as Apple CEO, John Ternus taking over
items.content: The transition has been expected for some time and ends one of the longer and more impactful runs a CEO has had at any company. Cook had inherited a company that many industry

items.title: The App Store is booming again, and AI may be why
items.content: New data from Appfigures shows a swell of new app launches in 2026, suggesting AI tools could be fueling a mobile software boom.

items.title: How vibe-coding app Anything is rebuilding after getting booted from the App Store twice
items.content: Anything is planning to launch a desktop companion app to aid mobile app development after its App Store removal.

items.title: Hackers are abusing unpatched Windows security flaws to hack into organizations

items.title: Microsoft is officially killing its Outlook Lite app next month
items.content: Launched in 2022, Outlook Lite is a lightweight version of the regular Outlook app, designed for Android phones with limited storage and regions with slower internet connectio

items.title: Microsoft is working on yet another OpenClaw-like agent
items.content: The new features would be geared toward enterprise customers, with better security controls than the famously risky open source OpenClaw agent.

items.title: WireGuard VPN developer can’t ship software updates after Microsoft locks account

items.title: A criminal inquiry into the Fed looms large over the Warsh hearing.
Trump Dismisses a Merger While Seeking Suitors for Spirit Airlines
items.content: As he rejected the idea of a merger of United Airlines and American Airlines, President Trump said someone, including possibly the federal government, should buy or help Spiri

items.title: A Year After U.S.A.I.D.’s Death, Fired Workers Find Few Jobs and Much Loss
items.content: People have plowed through savings, cashed out retirement funds and moved in with relatives. Former U.S.A.I.D. workers estimate that less than half have found full-time work.
